Author: Sergii Starodubtsev
Title: AI Pair Programming (and Why It Drains You)
Text:
AI Pair Programming (and Why It Drains You)
Blog Post : Sharing Experience (friend link 🔗 for this article)
Today I started the day with a small jog. So the day promised to be good. It’s not that often I wake up early and go outside for a jog — usually I do it closer to dark. In other words, I was fit “to do the right thing,” as they say.
I came up with a plan for how I would approach a task I was working on and present it to the client. I created files — specifications describing the business rules. The domain is simple enough that I could have implemented everything in a couple of hours without writing these MD files. But I challenged myself to go with the process, since it wasn’t risky. I know the domain well enough, so I decided to experiment: what if I go through it with the AI assistant’s help?
I have the corp accounts I can use with GPT. (It doesn’t really matter which — it could have been a plugin for the IDE, whatever).
I first created the specification manually, then refined it iteratively through a question-and-answer cycle with the AI assistant. I ended up with a nice-looking specification: business requirements, domain-driven design rules, constraints. Normally, I just write code right away, keeping the rules in mind as I go. But this time I went through the planning — mainly to feed the AI enough context.
So I created this MD file specification, then an additional file with a more detailed plan for implementation. Not big descriptions — more like pinpoints: save to database here, check existence there, update, validate, and so on. I scaffolded the project: placeholders, empty test files, structural framework. This way I could fill in the business rules later and also safeguard against mistakes the AI might make if it misunderstood me.
And it did happen before — the AI often misunderstood me and required many iterations before it caught on. Even GPT-5 or Anthropic’s models, none of them are perfect. So I spent maybe an hour preparing the specification and scaffolding — about half the time I would have needed to implement everything manually. I thought: once all this setup is done, the code generation will just fill the gaps and be smooth. I even gave the AI the full codebase as context. The domain is simple, a CRUD-like app with some translation-related specifics.
The problems started right away. Despite the specificity, it misinterpreted the specification. For example, I only needed to write to the database, but it added an S3 bucket update step. It generated unrelated code, and even after I removed the step from the specification, it kept trying to use it — like a bad memory that wouldn’t go away. I realized it might have been better to reset the context entirely instead of trying to push corrections into the old one. That mistake cost me an extra hour or two.
Then I started with the tests: setting up expected scenarios, generating code, and running it. But the data wasn’t being written to the database. The generated codebase was already large enough that debugging became harder. Instead of just figuring it out myself, I tried to guide the AI: “the data isn’t showing up in the database, fix it.” But it was lost. I had to delete everything and restart with a slower approach: just get some data into the database first. That alone took another one or two hours — already 200% more time than if I’d just done it manually.
After finally getting data into the database, I spent another 30–60 minutes verifying whether it was correct. This was tricky because the project itself doesn’t follow strict domain-driven design. Objects could be in inconsistent states — e.g., “in progress” but also “submitted.” The AI had access to all the files, rules, and behaviors, but still missed the point. Without internal validation, I had to manually check the correctness of the data.
Once I had reliable input data, I could progress with my tests: filling data, triggering actions, checking state changes. Some items should change, others shouldn’t, depending on the scenario. After about an hour and a half, I had code that worked more or less as intended. At that point, the day was already eight hours in.
Then came tuning. The AI had generated a function with 14 parameters and nested transfer objects. I decided to refactor using a command pattern with validation and factory methods. I tried guiding the AI through the refactor, but none of the models are good at it. I wasted another hour, then gave up and did it manually in about 30 minutes.
I deliberately didn’t go the “vibe coding” way. Instead, I went with a meticulously planned approach and caution — yet it still created a lot of extra work for me afterwards nevertheless.
Here’s the “funny meme” about that:
There’s definitely room for stepping on the same rake again and again. Isn’t that what we call evolution?
The law of large numbers and rapid prompts, tokens burning away,
GPT pausing to check the sanity of the requests (obviously :)) — while some still worry about adding ‘Please’ or ‘Thank you’ at the end of a prompt, when they should be worrying about being over-vibed.
Ah, that Friday vibe! That pic made my day — even before the day started.
Only when the structure was obvious did I let the AI fill in gaps. In the end, I worked over nine hours, with another 30 minutes of small refactors. By then I was using the AI more carefully — only for local changes within a file, never for moving files around. Renames and structural moves I did through the IDE. Otherwise, the AI would explode the task into endless queries, and if I had been paying per API call, it could have cost $20 just for moving files.
So I did it the old-school way. Finally, after ten exhausting hours, I finished the task and opened a pull request. But I wasn’t proud of the time or energy it consumed.
The energy mostly went into dialogues that aren’t supposed to be dialogues.
With AI, I keep forgetting, –it’s not actually thinking — it’s more like a very good search tool that scaffolds code (I keep reminding myself).
It’s not good at reasoning, not good at moving trivial things around. Ask it to do something and it will take 20 detours, leaving you nervous and drained.
You don’t notice it at first, but after hours, the exhaustion is real. In my case, I was so focused I even lost my voice — like a football fan shouting at the game, part of the action but never touching the ball.
Usually, I use a Pomodoro timer to avoid this kind of drain. Work 25 minutes, rest 5 or 10, repeat. But this time, I skipped cycles because I was too engaged.
The AI drags you in with the promise of “just one more ask,” and before you notice, you’ve lost your ground after half an hour of back-and-forth. That makes it a dangerous experience.
If you want to rush things with AI, you need to be cautious — because it drains more than it gives if you try to go fast.
At this point, I think it might be better to step back, rely on simple auto-completion tools in the IDE, and avoid asking AI to “think.” Sometimes two steps backward are safer than one step forward.
